([Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"): *Whatumoana*;[[1]](#cite_note-1) formerly **Canterbury Crusaders**) are a [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") professional [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") team based in [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch"), who compete in the [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby") competition. They are the most successful team in the competition's history and have won a total of 13 titles ([1998](/wiki/1998_Super_12_season "1998 Super 12 season"), [1999](/wiki/1999_Super_12_season "1999 Super 12 season"), [2000](/wiki/2000_Super_12_season "2000 Super 12 season"), [2002](/wiki/2002_Super_12_season "2002 Super 12 season"), [2005](/wiki/2005_Super_12_season "2005 Super 12 season"), [2006](/wiki/2006_Super_14_season "2006 Super 14 season"), [2008](/wiki/2008_Super_14_season "2008 Super 14 season"), [2017](/wiki/2017_Super_Rugby_season "2017 Super Rugby season"), [2018](/wiki/2018_Super_Rugby_season "2018 Super Rugby season"), [2019](/wiki/2019_Super_Rugby_season "2019 Super Rugby season"), [2022](/wiki/2022_Super_Rugby_Pacific_season "2022 Super Rugby Pacific season"), [2023](/wiki/2023_Super_Rugby_Pacific_season "2023 Super Rugby Pacific season") and [2025](/wiki/2025_Super_Rugby_Pacific_season "2025 Super Rugby Pacific season")) as well as two regionalised Super Rugby titles in [2020](/wiki/2020_Super_Rugby_Aotearoa_season "2020 Super Rugby Aotearoa season") and [2021](/wiki/2021_Super_Rugby_Aotearoa_season "2021 Super Rugby Aotearoa season").

The **Crusaders** ([Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"): *Whatumoana*;[[1]](#cite_note-1) formerly **Canterbury Crusaders**) are a [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") professional [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") team based in [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch"), who compete in the [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby") competition. They are the most successful team in the competition's history and have won a total of 13 titles ([1998](/wiki/1998_Super_12_season "1998 Super 12 season"), [1999](/wiki/1999_Super_12_season "1999 Super 12 season"), [2000](/wiki/2000_Super_12_season "2000 Super 12 season"), [2002](/wiki/2002_Super_12_season "2002 Super 12 season"), [2005](/wiki/2005_Super_12_season "2005 Super 12 season"), [2006](/wiki/2006_Super_14_season "2006 Super 14 season"), [2008](/wiki/2008_Super_14_season "2008 Super 14 season"), [2017](/wiki/2017_Super_Rugby_season "2017 Super Rugby season"), [2018](/wiki/2018_Super_Rugby_season "2018 Super Rugby season"), [2019](/wiki/2019_Super_Rugby_season "2019 Super Rugby season"), [2022](/wiki/2022_Super_Rugby_Pacific_season "2022 Super Rugby Pacific season"), [2023](/wiki/2023_Super_Rugby_Pacific_season "2023 Super Rugby Pacific season") and [2025](/wiki/2025_Super_Rugby_Pacific_season "2025 Super Rugby Pacific season")) as well as two regionalised Super Rugby titles in [2020](/wiki/2020_Super_Rugby_Aotearoa_season "2020 Super Rugby Aotearoa season") and [2021](/wiki/2021_Super_Rugby_Aotearoa_season "2021 Super Rugby Aotearoa season").

Formed in 1996 to represent the upper [South Island](/wiki/South_Island "South Island") of New Zealand in the Super 12, the Crusaders represent the [Buller](/wiki/Buller_Rugby_Football_Union "Buller Rugby Football Union"), [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury_Rugby_Football_Union "Canterbury Rugby Football Union"), [Mid-Canterbury](/wiki/Mid_Canterbury_Rugby_Football_Union "Mid Canterbury Rugby Football Union"), [South Canterbury](/wiki/South_Canterbury_Rugby_Football_Union "South Canterbury Rugby Football Union"), [Tasman](/wiki/Tasman_Rugby_Union "Tasman Rugby Union") and [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_Rugby_Football_Union "West Coast Rugby Football Union") provincial Rugby Unions. Their main home ground since 2012 is [Rugby League Park](/wiki/Rugby_League_Park "Rugby League Park") (known for sponsorship reasons as Apollo Projects Stadium).[[2]](#cite_note-2) They formerly played out of [Jade Stadium](/wiki/Lancaster_Park "Lancaster Park") prior to it being damaged beyond repair in the [2011 Christchurch earthquake](/wiki/2011_Christchurch_earthquake "2011 Christchurch earthquake").[[3]](#cite_note-StadiumJuly2011-3)[[4]](#cite_note-StadiumAugust2011-4)

The Crusaders struggled in the first season of the Super 12, [1996](/wiki/1996_Super_12_season "1996 Super 12 season"), finishing last. Their performance improved in [1997](/wiki/1997_Super_12_season "1997 Super 12 season") and the team finished sixth (out of twelve teams). The team went on to win three titles from [1998](/wiki/1998_Super_12_season "1998 Super 12 season") to [2000](/wiki/2000_Super_12_season "2000 Super 12 season") despite each final being played away from home. They again won the competition in [2002](/wiki/2002_Super_12_season "2002 Super 12 season") after going through the season unbeaten. In the following two seasons, they again reached the final, although they were beaten on both occasions. In [2005](/wiki/2005_Super_12_season "2005 Super 12 season"), it was the last season of the Super 12 before its expansion. After finishing top of the table in that season, the Crusaders went on to host the final in which they defeated the [Waratahs](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Waratahs "New South Wales Waratahs") (35–25). As a result of winning their fifth Super 12 title, the Crusaders were given the trophy to keep. In [2006](/wiki/2006_Super_14_season "2006 Super 14 season"), the Crusaders hosted the [Hurricanes](/wiki/Hurricanes_(rugby_union) "Hurricanes (rugby union)") in the inaugural [Super 14 final](/wiki/2006_Super_14_final "2006 Super 14 final") and won (19–12). In [2008](/wiki/2008_Super_14_final "2008 Super 14 final"), the Crusaders hosted the final at [AMI Stadium](/wiki/Lancaster_Park "Lancaster Park") against the Waratahs, and won the match (20–12) to claim their seventh title. Their eighth championship came in the [2017](/wiki/2017_Super_Rugby_final "2017 Super Rugby final") final when they defeated the [Lions](/wiki/Lions_(United_Rugby_Championship) "Lions (United Rugby Championship)") (25–17) in [Johannesburg](/wiki/Johannesburg "Johannesburg"). The Crusaders hosted the Lions in the [2018](/wiki/2018_Super_Rugby_final "2018 Super Rugby final") final and won (37–18), securing their ninth title. The Crusaders won their tenth Super Rugby title at home against the [Jaguares](/wiki/Jaguares_(Super_Rugby) "Jaguares (Super Rugby)") (19–3) in the [2019](/wiki/2019_Super_Rugby_final "2019 Super Rugby final") final. The Crusaders won their 11th and 12th title in both editions of [Super Rugby Aotearoa](/wiki/Super_Rugby_Aotearoa "Super Rugby Aotearoa") in [2020](/wiki/2020_Super_Rugby_Aotearoa_season "2020 Super Rugby Aotearoa season") and [2021](/wiki/2021_Super_Rugby_Aotearoa_season "2021 Super Rugby Aotearoa season"). They won their 11th full title (13th overall) by winning the inaugural [Super Rugby Pacific](/wiki/2022_Super_Rugby_Pacific_season "2022 Super Rugby Pacific season") final in [2022](/wiki/2022_Super_Rugby_Pacific_final "2022 Super Rugby Pacific final") against the [Blues](/wiki/Blues_(Super_Rugby) "Blues (Super Rugby)") (21–7) at [Eden Park](/wiki/Eden_Park "Eden Park") in [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"). The Crusaders won their 12th full Super Rugby title (14th overall) after beating the [Chiefs](/wiki/Chiefs_(Super_Rugby) "Chiefs (Super Rugby)") (25–20) in the [2023](/wiki/2023_Super_Rugby_Pacific_final "2023 Super Rugby Pacific final") final in [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_New_Zealand "Hamilton, New Zealand"). The Crusaders won their 13th full title (15th overall) after beating the [Chiefs](/wiki/Chiefs_(Super_Rugby) "Chiefs (Super Rugby)") (16–12) in the [2025](/wiki/2025_Super_Rugby_Pacific_final "2025 Super Rugby Pacific final") final in [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch").

The Crusaders have been led by only three CEOs throughout their 29-year history; former [New Zealand Rugby](/wiki/New_Zealand_Rugby "New Zealand Rugby") CEO [Steve Tew](/wiki/Steve_Tew "Steve Tew"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) Hamish Riach[[6]](#cite_note-6) and incumbent Colin Mansbridge.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Crusaders_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Beginnings: 1996–1997

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Crusaders_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Beginnings: 1996–1997")]

The Crusaders franchise was created as one of five New Zealand teams in the Super 12. Originally named the *Canterbury Crusaders*, the Crusaders' franchise area encompassed the upper [South Island](/wiki/South_Island "South Island") of New Zealand (see *[Super Rugby franchise areas](/wiki/Super_Rugby_franchise_areas "Super Rugby franchise areas")*), and was formed from the Buller, Canterbury, [Marlborough](/wiki/Marlborough_Rugby_Union "Marlborough Rugby Union"), Mid-Canterbury, [Nelson Bays](/wiki/Nelson_Bays_Rugby_Union "Nelson Bays Rugby Union"), South Canterbury, and West Coast rugby unions.[[8]](#cite_note-McIlraith320-8)[[a]](#cite_note-9) The original Crusaders team of 1996 was captained by [All Blacks](/wiki/All_Blacks "All Blacks") [prop](/wiki/Rugby_union_positions#1._Loosehead_prop_.26_3._Tighthead_prop "Rugby union positions") [Richard Loe](/wiki/Richard_Loe "Richard Loe") and coached by Vance Stewart.[[9]](#cite_note-McIlraith321-10) The Crusaders struggled in the inaugural season and finished bottom of the table with only two wins.[[10]](#cite_note-McIlraith235-11) Their eight losses included a 49–18 loss to the [Blues](/wiki/Blues_(Super_Rugby) "Blues (Super Rugby)") and a 52–16 loss to the [Queensland Reds](/wiki/Queensland_Reds "Queensland Reds").[[11]](#cite_note-Gifford151-12) And the First ever tour was a pre-visit to South Africa prior start of Super 12.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

The following season saw a change in captain and coach, with [Todd Blackadder](/wiki/Todd_Blackadder "Todd Blackadder") succeeding Loe as captain and [Wayne Smith](/wiki/Wayne_Smith_(rugby_union) "Wayne Smith (rugby union)") taking over as coach.[[9]](#cite_note-McIlraith321-10) With five wins, the team finished the round-robin stage in sixth place.[[13]](#cite_note-McIlraith243-14) The improvement was particularly illustrated by the Crusaders' 29–28 loss to the defending champions, the Blues, which contrasted with their 49–18 loss the previous season. (The Blues also went on to win the 1997 title).[[14]](#cite_note-Gifford159-15) During this loss, [Leon MacDonald](/wiki/Leon_MacDonald "Leon MacDonald") was taken out with a shoulder charge by Robin Brooke. While the Crusaders attempted to get MacDonald back on the field, the Blues scored two tries, including one by Brooke, who was later suspended for two weeks for the shoulder charge.[[14]](#cite_note-Gifford159-15) In their last game of 1997, the Crusaders beat the Queensland Reds 48–3 at [Lancaster Park](/wiki/Lancaster_Park "Lancaster Park").[[13]](#cite_note-McIlraith243-14)

### Hat trick: 1998–2000

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Crusaders_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Hat trick: 1998–2000")]

The Crusaders won their first title in 1998, despite starting the season with three losses in their first four games. They finished the [round-robin](/wiki/Round-robin_tournament "Round-robin tournament") by winning their last seven games, culminating in a last-round win over the Coastal Sharks (now the [Sharks](/wiki/Sharks_(rugby_union) "Sharks (rugby union)")) that gave them second place in the round-robin phase of the competition.[[15]](#cite_note-Gifford169-16) Their second-placing allowed them to host their semi-final at Lancaster Park, where they won the match 36–32 against the Coastal Sharks.[[16]](#cite_note-McIlraith252-17)

In the final at [Eden Park](/wiki/Eden_Park "Eden Park"), the Crusaders faced the Blues, who were heavy favourites. According to Crusaders' hooker [Mark Hammett](/wiki/Mark_Hammett "Mark Hammett"), "If we'd been polled in that week, and had to give an honest answer, most of the boys, deep down, would probably have thought that the Blues would beat us."[[17]](#cite_note-McIlraith84-18) The Crusaders were ahead 3–0 at half time, but the Blues scored first after half time to take a 10–3 lead after 53 minutes.[[18]](#cite_note-Gifford171-19) After Crusader [Norm Maxwell](/wiki/Norm_Maxwell "Norm Maxwell") scored a try, the game was tied 10 all. Then, after a [penalty](/wiki/Penalty_(rugby) "Penalty (rugby)") each, the two teams were tied 13 all with one minute of regulation time remaining.[[19]](#cite_note-Gifford172-20) At that moment, Andrew Mehrtens chipped the ball for [James Kerr](/wiki/James_Kerr_(rugby_union) "James Kerr (rugby union)") to run onto and score, giving the Crusaders a 20–13 win after the try was [converted](/wiki/Try_(rugby) "Try (rugby)").[[20]](#cite_note-McIlraith85-21) The ten points scored by Andrew Mehrtens in the final contributed to his total of 206 points for the season − a record for the Super 12.[[21]](#cite_note-McIlraith335-22) Upon the Crusaders' return to Christchurch, they were given a parade through the city that drew 100,000 people.[[19]](#cite_note-Gifford172-20)

> We're a regional selection... and it's important to remember that, besides Canterbury, we also represent Nelson Bays, Marlborough, Buller, West Coast, Mid-Canterbury and South Canterbury

— [Todd Blackadder](/wiki/Todd_Blackadder "Todd Blackadder"), Crusaders captain, 1998 Super 12 victory speech[[22]](#cite_note-VictorySpeech-23)

The 1999 season was equally successful for the Crusaders despite struggling to make the play-offs in fourth place after winning their last four round-robin matches.[[23]](#cite_note-Gifford174-24)[[24]](#cite_note-McIlraith260-25) They defeated the favoured Queensland Reds (who had finished top in the round-robin) in their semi-final to advance to the final against the [Otago Highlanders](/wiki/Highlanders_(rugby_union) "Highlanders (rugby union)") (since renamed the *Highlanders*).[[24]](#cite_note-McIlraith260-25) The all-[South Island](/wiki/South_Island "South Island") final in [Dunedin](/wiki/Dunedin "Dunedin") was promoted as "the party at Tony Brown's house" after Highlanders [first five-eighths](/wiki/Fly-half_(rugby_union) "Fly-half (rugby union)") [Tony Brown](/wiki/Tony_Brown_(rugby_union) "Tony Brown (rugby union)").[[23]](#cite_note-Gifford174-24) Again playing away from home, the Crusaders won 24–19. The decisive try of the match was scored by Crusaders [winger](/wiki/Wing_(rugby_union) "Wing (rugby union)") [Afato So'oalo](/wiki/Afato_So%27oalo "Afato So'oalo") when he chipped the ball, then out-sprinted All Blacks winger [Jeff Wilson](/wiki/Jeff_Wilson_(sportsman) "Jeff Wilson (sportsman)") to collect the ball and score.[[25]](#cite_note-Gifford175-26)[[26]](#cite_note-McIlraith106-27)

Following the All Blacks' semi-final loss in the 1999 [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup"), [Wayne Smith](/wiki/Wayne_Smith_(rugby_union) "Wayne Smith (rugby union)") succeeded [John Hart](/wiki/John_Hart_(rugby_coach) "John Hart (rugby coach)") as All Blacks coach.[[27]](#cite_note-Polenski232-28) Crusaders manager [Robbie Deans](/wiki/Robbie_Deans "Robbie Deans") replaced Smith as Crusaders coach. In Deans' first year in charge, the Crusaders won their third title in a row − a feat that would be repeated in the 2017-2018-2019 seasons.[[9]](#cite_note-McIlraith321-10) The Crusaders finished second in the round-robin, earning them the right to host a semi-final in which they faced the Highlanders at Jade Stadium.[[28]](#cite_note-McIlraith268-29)[[29]](#cite_note-McIlraith269-30) Two tries in the final 20 minutes by [Marika Vunibaka](/wiki/Marika_Vunibaka "Marika Vunibaka") helped the Crusaders to a 37–15 win.[[30]](#cite_note-McIlraith124-31) The 2000 [Super 12](/wiki/Super_12 "Super 12") Final was played against the [Brumbies](/wiki/Brumbies_Rugby "Brumbies Rugby") in [Canberra, Australia](/wiki/Canberra,_Australia "Canberra, Australia"), where the weather for the final was icy, with sleet and snow.[[31]](#cite_note-Gifford179-32) The game was low-scoring, with only one try each. Four penalties by Mehrtens gave the Crusaders a 12–6 half time lead. Following a Brumbies penalty four minutes from time, the Brumbies led 19–17.[[31]](#cite_note-Gifford179-32) However, the Crusaders were awarded a penalty from the resulting kick-off, and after Mehrtens converted the penalty, the Crusaders went on to win the game 20–19.[[32]](#cite_note-McIlraith125-33)

### Development of a legacy: 2001–2005

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Crusaders_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development of a legacy: 2001–2005")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Scrum.JPG/250px-Scrum.JPG)](/wiki/File:Scrum.JPG)

Crusaders [scrum](/wiki/Scrum_(rugby) "Scrum (rugby)") against the Brumbies in May 2006

After winning three consecutive Super 12 titles, the Crusaders finished tenth in 2001 – their worst finish since 1996. The season was the last for captain Todd Blackadder before he left to play for [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby") in Scotland.[[33]](#cite_note-Blackadder2001-34)[[b]](#cite_note-35)

The Crusaders bounced back dramatically in 2002 when they went through the Super 12 season unbeaten, achieving their fourth title. Although the Crusaders won all eleven of their round-robin matches, six of them were won by a margin of seven points or less.[[c]](#cite_note-36)[[34]](#cite_note-McIlraith164-37) Their eleven round-robin games also included a record 96–19 win over the Waratahs in the final match of the round-robin stage, which was especially noteworthy given that both teams finished at the top of the table.

The Crusaders met the Highlanders in their 2002 semi-final, which they eventually won 34–23 after being ahead 18–6 at half time. The following week, they faced the Brumbies in the first final to be hosted at Jade Stadium. The Brumbies trailed 11–3 after the first half, having had only 30 per cent possession.[[35]](#cite_note-Gifford189-38) Although the Brumbies closed the gap to 14–13 with eight minutes to go, the Crusaders held on and steadied to win 31–13 with a last-minute try to [Caleb Ralph](/wiki/Caleb_Ralph "Caleb Ralph").[[35]](#cite_note-Gifford189-38)[[36]](#cite_note-McIlraith165-39) The season was their first under the captaincy of [Reuben Thorne](/wiki/Reuben_Thorne "Reuben Thorne"), who went on to captain the All Blacks until the end of the [2003 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2003_Rugby_World_Cup "2003 Rugby World Cup").

The Crusaders' winning run finally came to an end when they were beaten 39–5 by the Blues in the third round of the 2003 Super 12 season.[[37]](#cite_note-McIlraith288-40) Although their 15-match unbeaten streak had ended, Richie McCaw said of the loss, "...in some ways it was almost a relief. We'd finally been beaten, the run was over, so people could stop talking about it and we could get on with playing it week by week."[[38]](#cite_note-McIlraith170-41) The Crusaders recovered to finish second on the table with eight wins.[[39]](#cite_note-McIlraith294-42) They hosted a semi-final against the Hurricanes, who were coached by Colin Cooper, a former assistant coach for the Crusaders. Despite this inside knowledge of the team by the Wellington coach, the Crusaders won the match 39–16.[[39]](#cite_note-McIlraith294-42) In the final, the Crusaders met the Blues, another team coached by a former Crusaders assistant coach, [Peter Sloane](/wiki/Peter_Sloane "Peter Sloane"). Hooker [Mark Hammett](/wiki/Mark_Hammett "Mark Hammett") scored two tries to give the Crusaders a 10–6 lead at half-time.[[40]](#cite_note-McIlraith186-43) The Blues hit back to lead 21–10 with ten minutes to go. The Crusaders managed another converted try, but it was not enough, and the Blues won 21–17.[[40]](#cite_note-McIlraith186-43)

The Crusaders' 2004 season began with two losses: first to the Waratahs, then to the Blues.[[41]](#cite_note-McIlraith191-44) They recovered to again finish second on the table with seven wins.[[42]](#cite_note-McIlraith302-45) They played the Stormers at Jade Stadium in their semi-final, and won 27–16.[[42]](#cite_note-McIlraith302-45) They met the Brumbies at [Canberra Stadium](/wiki/Canberra_Stadium "Canberra Stadium") in a replay of the 2000 final. The Brumbies dominated in the final, scoring 33 unanswered points in the first 19 minutes.[[43]](#cite_note-McIlraith188-46) The Crusaders managed to salvage some pride by narrowing the loss to a 47–38 finish.[[42]](#cite_note-McIlraith302-45)

The 2005 season started with a repeat of the previous year's final: a Brumbies victory over the Crusaders in Canberra.[[44]](#cite_note-McIlraith303-47) There was a win the following week at home over the [Chiefs](/wiki/Chiefs_(Super_Rugby) "Chiefs (Super Rugby)"), followed by a win over the Reds at [Trafalgar Park](/wiki/Trafalgar_Park,_Nelson "Trafalgar Park, Nelson").[[45]](#cite_note-McIlraith305-48) Although there was a 35–20 loss to the Bulls after Crusaders captain Richie McCaw was knocked unconscious, they eventually finished the regular season top of the table.[[46]](#cite_note-McIlraith203-49)[[47]](#cite_note-McIlraith310-50) McCaw returned from his five weeks on the sideline to lead the Crusaders in their semi-final victory over the Hurricanes.[[46]](#cite_note-McIlraith203-49)[[47]](#cite_note-McIlraith310-50) They faced the Waratahs in the final at Jade Stadium.
Leading 14–6 at halftime, the Crusaders then moved out to a 29-point lead.[[48]](#cite_note-McIlraith205-51) Despite three late tries by the Waratahs, the Crusaders won their fifth title with a 35–20 win.[[48]](#cite_note-McIlraith205-51)[[49]](#cite_note-McIlraith311-52)

As a reward for their seventh finals appearance and fifth title, the Crusaders were allowed to keep the Super 12 trophy.[[50]](#cite_note-KeepTrophy-53) After the 2005 season, the franchise saw the departure of stalwarts [Andrew Mehrtens](/wiki/Andrew_Mehrtens "Andrew Mehrtens") and [Justin Marshall](/wiki/Justin_Marshall "Justin Marshall"), both of whom had played for the team since its formation in 1996.

### Super 14: 2006–2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Crusaders_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Super 14: 2006–2010")]

Further information: [2006 Super 14 season](/wiki/2006_Super_14_season "2006 Super 14 season"), [2006 Super 14 Final](/wiki/2006_Super_14_Final "2006 Super 14 Final")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Lineout.JPG/250px-Lineout.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lineout.JPG)

The Crusaders playing the Bulls in 2006.

In 2006, the Super 12 expanded to form the Super 14 when the [Western Force](/wiki/Western_Force "Western Force") from [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth"), Australia, and the [Cheetahs](/wiki/Cheetahs_(rugby_union) "Cheetahs (rugby union)") from the [Free State](/wiki/Free_State_Province "Free State Province") and [Northern Cape Provinces](/wiki/Northern_Cape_Province "Northern Cape Province"), South Africa joined the competition. An unbeaten run of sixteen matches was nearly halted when the Crusaders drew with the Western Force in Perth in round eleven.[[51]](#cite_note-Round11Review-54) The following week, the unbeaten run was ended when the Crusaders lost to the Stormers in Cape Town.[[52]](#cite_note-Super14Tournament-55) That loss was their only one of the year, as they finished top of the table with eleven wins.[[53]](#cite_note-06S14Table-56) After beating the Bulls in their semi-final, the Crusaders faced the Hurricanes in the final at Jade Stadium that was played in a thick fog, causing poor visibility.[[54]](#cite_note-S14Final-57) Sections of the crowd could not see the field, and many spectators left during the match.[[54]](#cite_note-S14Final-57) The scorers were tied 9–9 going into the game's last 20 minutes. The Crusaders' [Casey Laulala](/wiki/Casey_Laulala "Casey Laulala") then scored the game's only try before the match finished 19–12.[[54]](#cite_note-S14Final-57)

In the [2007 Super 14](/wiki/2007_Super_14 "2007 Super 14") season, seven Crusaders players missed the first seven rounds of the competition to participate in an All Black "conditioning programme".[[55]](#cite_note-2007Conditioning-58) The conditioning programme was part of the All Blacks' [2007 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2007_Rugby_World_Cup "2007 Rugby World Cup") preparations; 22 players were selected for the programme. The Crusaders' seven players in the programme was more than any other New Zealand Super 14 franchise. The Crusaders players were [Chris Jack](/wiki/Chris_Jack "Chris Jack"), Richie McCaw, Greg Somerville, Reuben Thorne, Dan Carter, Leon MacDonald, and [Aaron Mauger](/wiki/Aaron_Mauger "Aaron Mauger").[[55]](#cite_note-2007Conditioning-58)[[56]](#cite_note-ConditioningSquad-59)[[57]](#cite_note-ConditioningSquad2-60) All but one of the seven returned to play for the Crusaders in their eighth round match against the Stormers (Greg Somerville did not return due to injury).[[58]](#cite_note-ReconditionedReturn-61) The Crusaders climbed to second on the table before defeating the [Western Force](/wiki/Western_Force "Western Force") 53–0 at Jade Stadium in the tenth round.[[59]](#cite_note-Force07-62) The victory over the Force was the one hundredth Super rugby win for the Crusaders – the first franchise to reach the milestone.[[59]](#cite_note-Force07-62)[[d]](#cite_note-63)

The Crusaders were top of the table going into the last round of the Super 14 before the play-offs. To guarantee a home semi-final they needed to defeat the Chiefs at Jade Stadium. The Chiefs won 30–24 – and ended the Crusaders 26 game winning streak at home.[[60]](#cite_note-26GameOver-64) As the Bulls defeated the Reds 92–3 in the same round, they overtook the Crusaders to finish second in the round robin.[[61]](#cite_note-BullsReds923-65) The Crusaders then travelled to Pretoria to face the Bulls in their semi-final where they lost 27–12.[[62]](#cite_note-Semi07-66)

The 2008 season was another highly successful one for the Crusaders. After finishing top of the table at the end of the last round,[[63]](#cite_note-Top42008-67) they comfortably defeated the Hurricanes 33–22 in the semi-final,[[64]](#cite_note-2008Semi-68) and went on to beat the Waratahs in the final 20–12.[[65]](#cite_note-2008Final-69) It was a fitting way to send off long-serving coach Robbie Deans before he departed to coach [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_union_team "Australia national rugby union team").[[66]](#cite_note-DeansLeaving-70) It was also the last game for Crusaders stalwarts Caleb Ralph and former captain Reuben Thorne.[[67]](#cite_note-ThorneRalph-71) In July that year former captain [Todd Blackadder](/wiki/Todd_Blackadder "Todd Blackadder") was appointed Crusaders coach, and his former teammates [Mark Hammett](/wiki/Mark_Hammett "Mark Hammett") and [Daryl Gibson](/wiki/Daryl_Gibson "Daryl Gibson") were appointed as assistants.[[68]](#cite_note-BlackadderAppointed-72)

The 2009 season started with a sole win and a draw from the Crusaders' first five matches; this included a 6–0 loss to the Highlanders.[[69]](#cite_note-FirstFive2009-73)[[70]](#cite_note-HighlandersLoss2009-74) The side found its feet later in the season, and won key away games which ultimately saw them finish in fourth place on the table – just ahead of the Waratahs.[[71]](#cite_note-RegularSeason2009-75) Both the Crusaders and Waratahs finished the season with 41 competition points, but the Crusaders advanced through to the semi-finals due to a greater 'points for and against' difference.

The Crusaders lost their semi-final to the [Bulls](/wiki/Bulls_(rugby_franchise) "Bulls (rugby franchise)") 36–23 at [Loftus Versfeld](/wiki/Loftus_Versfeld "Loftus Versfeld"), [Pretoria](/wiki/Pretoria "Pretoria") in front of a capacity 52,000 crowd.[[72]](#cite_note-2009Semi-76) The Bulls went on to win the 2009 Super 14 title beating the Chiefs 61–17.[[73]](#cite_note-ChiefsBullsFinal-77) Statistically, the Crusaders had the best defensive record of the 2009 competition – conceding just 198 points. However, points for, and total tries scored was the second lowest in the competition with just 231 points and 27 tries.

In 2010 the Crusaders had a very good start to the season before a poor finish with three losses in four games caused them to have to travel to [Orlando Stadium](/wiki/Orlando_Stadium "Orlando Stadium"), [Soweto](/wiki/Soweto "Soweto") to face the [Bulls](/wiki/Bulls_(rugby_franchise) "Bulls (rugby franchise)"). They lost the semi-final 39–24.

### 2011–2016

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Crusaders_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2011–2016")]

The 2011 season had a minor hiccup at the very beginning with a two-point loss to the [Blues](/wiki/Blues_(Super_Rugby) "Blues (Super Rugby)"). Their second round match against the [Hurricanes](/wiki/Hurricanes_(rugby_union) "Hurricanes (rugby union)") was cancelled, and match declared a draw after the [Christchurch earthquake](/wiki/2011_Christchurch_earthquake "2011 Christchurch earthquake") on 22 February which caused 182 deaths. In their first match after the earthquake they played the [Waratahs](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Waratahs "New South Wales Waratahs"), a team that had only conceded six points in their previous two games. The Crusaders triumphed 33–18 in a convincing display in front of a sold-out stadium. They next played the [Brumbies](/wiki/ACT_Brumbies "ACT Brumbies") and brushed them aside in a 52–10 demolition. They next played the [Highlanders](/wiki/Highlanders_(rugby_union) "Highlanders (rugby union)") who had previously only lost one match in the season, to the undefeated and table-topping [Stormers](/wiki/Stormers "Stormers"). The Crusaders won comfortably 44–13 in Dunedin. The next team they played, the [Sharks](/wiki/Sharks_(rugby_union) "Sharks (rugby union)"), had like the Highlanders previously only lost one match in the season before the Crusaders won 44–28 at [Twickenham](/wiki/Twickenham_stadium "Twickenham stadium") for a one-off match due to earthquake damage at their own ground. They played the second match in a sell-out crowd at [Fraser Park](/wiki/Fraser_Park_(Timaru) "Fraser Park (Timaru)") in Timaru against the Bulls winning 27–0. Round 9 they played the Chiefs in Mt Maunganui and gained four tries winning 34–16 with Richie McCaw returning after eight weeks off with injury.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]Towards the end of the round-robin phase, The Crusaders were vying with the Queensland Reds for top spot in the table and with it home advantage throughout the playoffs. In the crucial deciding match v the [Queensland Reds](/wiki/Queensland_Reds "Queensland Reds") in Brisbane, the Crusaders had a narrow lead going into the final minute when the Australian referee awarded the Reds a highly controversial penalty and match-winning opportunity. This was duly converted, handing the Reds the aforementioned home-field playoff advantage. The Crusaders lost the final at the Suncorp Stadium to the [Queensland Reds](/wiki/Queensland_Reds "Queensland Reds"). Will Genia scored a crucial try for the Reds to help them to an 18–13 win.[[74]](#cite_note-78)

The 2012 season, the first in the new home ground of [Christchurch Stadium](/wiki/Christchurch_Stadium "Christchurch Stadium"), saw the Crusaders again reach the playoffs. However, they were defeated by the (eventual champion) [Chiefs](/wiki/Chiefs_(Super_Rugby) "Chiefs (Super Rugby)") in the semi-final.

### 2017–2023

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Crusaders_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2017–2023")]

In 2017, Scott Robertson took over as head coach. The Crusaders lost 3–12 to the [British & Irish Lions](/wiki/British_%26_Irish_Lions "British & Irish Lions") in [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch"), during the [Lions tour to New Zealand](/wiki/2017_British_%26_Irish_Lions_tour_to_New_Zealand "2017 British & Irish Lions tour to New Zealand").[[75]](#cite_note-79)

The team went on to win their 8th Super Rugby title, defeating the [Lions](/wiki/Lions_(United_Rugby_Championship) "Lions (United Rugby Championship)") (25–17) in the final on 5 August 2017 at [Ellis Park Stadium](/wiki/Ellis_Park_Stadium "Ellis Park Stadium") in [Johannesburg](/wiki/Johannesburg "Johannesburg").[[76]](#cite_note-80)

In 2018, the Crusaders played the Lions again in the final. This time they won (37–18) at home to capture their ninth title.[[77]](#cite_note-81)

In 2019, they defeated the [Jaguares](/wiki/Jaguares_(Super_Rugby) "Jaguares (Super Rugby)") from [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") (19–3) at home to win their 3rd consecutive Super Rugby title, and 10th championship overall.[[78]](#cite_note-82)

After 7 rounds of the 2020 Super Rugby season, the Crusaders were leading the NZ conference and were in 3rd overall (behind the [Sharks](/wiki/Sharks_(rugby_union) "Sharks (rugby union)") and [Brumbies](/wiki/ACT_Brumbies "ACT Brumbies")) but the COVID-19 pandemic stopped the competition after the 7th round.[[79]](#cite_note-83) However, in June 2020 a domestic Super Rugby competition started called [Super Rugby Aotearoa](/wiki/Super_Rugby_Aotearoa "Super Rugby Aotearoa"). The Crusaders won the overall title in Super Rugby Aotearoa 2020, winning 7 out of 8 games.[[80]](#cite_note-84) Their 36-game win streak at Orangetheory Stadium was broken however by the [Hurricanes](/wiki/Hurricanes_(rugby_union) "Hurricanes (rugby union)") in Round 7, who defeated them (32–34).[[81]](#cite_note-85) The Crusaders dropped the physical trophy in post-match celebrations, chipping the [pounamu](/wiki/Pounamu "Pounamu") *[mere](/wiki/Mere_(weapon) "Mere (weapon)")* that was on it.[[82]](#cite_note-86)

The Crusaders returned to Super Rugby Aotearoa in 2021. Despite losses to the [Highlanders](/wiki/Highlanders_(rugby_union) "Highlanders (rugby union)") (12–33) and the [Chiefs](/wiki/Chiefs_(Super_Rugby) "Chiefs (Super Rugby)") (25–26), they went on to beat the Chiefs in the final and win their 5th consecutive title in a (24–13) victory.[[83]](#cite_note-87) Following that, they went into the Super Rugby Trans Tasman competition and beat the Brumbies (31–29), [Reds](/wiki/Queensland_Reds "Queensland Reds") (63–28), [Waratahs](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Waratahs "New South Wales Waratahs") (54–28), [Force](/wiki/Western_Force "Western Force") (29–21) and [Rebels](/wiki/Melbourne_Rebels "Melbourne Rebels") (52–26) but came up just short on points difference needed to be able to make the final.[[84]](#cite_note-88)

In 2022 and 2023, the Crusaders won their 11th and 12th full Super Rugby title (14th Overall) in the newly formed Super Rugby Pacific competition by beating the [Blues](/wiki/Blues_(Super_Rugby) "Blues (Super Rugby)") (21–7) and the Chiefs (25–20).[[85]](#cite_note-89)[[86]](#cite_note-90) At the end of the 2023 Grand Final, the Scott Robertson dynasty was complete and it marked the end of an era for Crusader #26 as a former player and as head coach.[[87]](#cite_note-91)

### 2024–

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Crusaders_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2024–")]

In June 2023, [Rob Penney](/wiki/Rob_Penney "Rob Penney") was announced as the Crusaders Head Coach for 2024 and 2025.[[88]](#cite_note-coaching-92) After winning 7 consecutive titles, the Crusaders slumped to a 4 win and 10 loss season in 2024 and missed the playoffs for the first time since 2015.[[89]](#cite_note-93) After a thorough review of an abysmal 2024 season, the Crusaders decided to keep Penney as Head Coach for 2025 and will finish out the remainder of his 2-year contract with the team.[[90]](#cite_note-94) In 2025, the Crusaders had a resurgence year. They finished the regular season in 2nd place by winning 11 from 14 games and finished within the top 6. After missing the playoffs in the previous year, the Crusaders qualified and went on to host the [Reds](/wiki/Queensland_Reds "Queensland Reds") in the qualifying final at home and defeated them (32–12) in a dominant victory.[[91]](#cite_note-95)[[92]](#cite_note-96) They advanced to the semi-final and hosted the defending champions the [Blues](/wiki/Blues_(Super_Rugby) "Blues (Super Rugby)") after they beat the top seeded Chiefs in their qualifying final game in [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_New_Zealand "Hamilton, New Zealand").[[93]](#cite_note-97)[[94]](#cite_note-98) With the Chiefs losing, the Crusaders jumped into the number one seed spot.[[95]](#cite_note-99) They defeated the Blues (21–14) after defending for over 40 phases and holding out the reigning champs to advance to the final.[[96]](#cite_note-100)[[97]](#cite_note-101) The Crusaders hosted the [Chiefs](/wiki/Chiefs_(Super_Rugby) "Chiefs (Super Rugby)") in the [Final](/wiki/2025_Super_Rugby_Pacific_final "2025 Super Rugby Pacific final") and defeated them (16–12) to win their 13th full title (15th overall) in [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch").[[98]](#cite_note-102)[[99]](#cite_note-103)

## Name and colours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Crusaders_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Name and colours")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Horsemen.JPG/250px-Horsemen.JPG)](/wiki/File:Horsemen.JPG)

The Horsemen performing to the crowd before a match

According to the club, the Crusaders name was chosen to reflect the "crusading nature of Canterbury rugby".[[100]](#cite_note-104) A potential name was also the *Plainsmen*; however, this was not chosen, as "Crusaders" recalled the English heritage of the city of Christchurch.

The club adopted Canterbury's traditional sporting colours of red and black, as worn by [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury_Rugby_Football_Union "Canterbury Rugby Football Union").

In the aftermath of the 15 March 2019 [Christchurch mosque shootings](/wiki/Christchurch_mosque_shootings "Christchurch mosque shootings") at [Al Noor Mosque](/wiki/Al_Noor_Mosque,_Christchurch "Al Noor Mosque, Christchurch") and [Linwood Islamic Centre](/wiki/Linwood_Islamic_Centre "Linwood Islamic Centre") in [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") which killed 51 people and injured 50 more, the Crusaders considered the idea changing the team name because the symbolism and imagery was considered to be "offensive to some in the community due to its association with the religious [Crusades](/wiki/The_Crusades "The Crusades") between [Christians](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") and [Muslims](/wiki/Islam "Islam")" according to New Zealand Rugby Executive Steve Tew but ultimately though it was kept and remained unchanged for the 2020 season.[[101]](#cite_note-105)[[102]](#cite_note-106) The Crusaders management led by Colin Mansbridge, revealed a new logo to replace the knight and sword in late 2019.[[103]](#cite_note-107)

## Franchise area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Crusaders_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Franchise area")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/CrusadersTerritory.PNG/250px-CrusadersTerritory.PNG)](/wiki/File:CrusadersTerritory.PNG "The six unions which represent the Crusaders and the three home grounds")

⊗ [AMI Stadium](/wiki/Lancaster_Park "Lancaster Park")

⊗ [Fraser Park](/wiki/Fraser_Park_(Timaru) "Fraser Park (Timaru)")

⊗ [Trafalgar Park](/wiki/Trafalgar_Park,_Nelson "Trafalgar Park, Nelson")

The Crusaders franchise area consists of the regions controlled by the Buller, Canterbury, Mid-Canterbury, South Canterbury, Tasman and West Coast rugby unions.[[104]](#cite_note-108) Before 2006, when the [NPC](/wiki/National_Provincial_Championship_(1976%E2%80%932005) "National Provincial Championship (1976–2005)") was reorganised, Canterbury was the only union playing division one (now [Bunnings NPC](/wiki/Bunnings_NPC "Bunnings NPC")) within the Crusaders catchment. As a result, the majority of Crusaders players were also listed with Canterbury. Today, the Crusaders catchment contains two Bunnings NPC sides – Canterbury and Tasman.

## Grounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Crusaders_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Grounds")]

The Crusaders' main home ground was the 36,000-capacity AMI Stadium in Christchurch.[[105]](#cite_note-stadiumcapacity-109) However, they stopped playing there during the 2011 Super Rugby season due to damage inflicted by the February Christchurch earthquake. The Crusaders usually took one game each year to another part of the franchise area, most often when the [New Zealand cricket team](/wiki/New_Zealand_cricket_team "New Zealand cricket team") was playing at AMI Stadium.[[106]](#cite_note-TimaruGame-110) Previous venues included Trafalgar Park in [Nelson](/wiki/Nelson,_New_Zealand "Nelson, New Zealand"), and Fraser Park in [Timaru](/wiki/Timaru "Timaru").[[106]](#cite_note-TimaruGame-110)[[107]](#cite_note-NelsonGame-111)

With AMI Stadium still out of commission, the Crusaders adopted [Rugby League Park](/wiki/Rugby_League_Park "Rugby League Park") (which has been renamed Christchurch Stadium temporarily) in [Addington](/wiki/Addington,_New_Zealand "Addington, New Zealand") as their home ground for that and future seasons to recentralise their games in one location as opposed to the road show that was the 2011 season. The ground had a limited makeover, taking it to an 18,600 capacity stadium and bringing its facilities to the minimum standards required by the Super Rugby competition.[[108]](#cite_note-112)

The Crusaders' management proposed playing a 2005 regular season match in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne") when Jade Stadium was unavailable.[[109]](#cite_note-NoMelbourne-113) Although five of the seven unions within the Crusaders region[[e]](#cite_note-114) supported having the regular season game in Melbourne, the [New Zealand Rugby Union](/wiki/New_Zealand_Rugby_Union "New Zealand Rugby Union") (NZRU) vetoed the proposal.[[109]](#cite_note-NoMelbourne-113) In 2006, the Crusaders did play a pre-season match in Melbourne against the Western Force at [Olympic Park Stadium](/wiki/Olympic_Park_Stadium_(Melbourne) "Olympic Park Stadium (Melbourne)").[[110]](#cite_note-MelbourneHost-115) They did the same in the 2008 preseason as well, also against the Force.

Due to the 2011 earthquake, the Crusaders played four games at Trafalgar Park, and two in Timaru during their 2011 season. They also played a "home" game in Wellington in Round 18 against Hurricanes, and a special home match against the [Sharks](/wiki/Sharks_(rugby_union) "Sharks (rugby union)") at [Twickenham](/wiki/Twickenham_Stadium "Twickenham Stadium") in London. The London match was a fundraiser for earthquake relief, and was the first Super Rugby match ever contested outside of the three participating countries.[[111]](#cite_note-116) In the first 30 hours of ticket sales, over 30,000 were purchased; a spokesman for [United Ticketmaster](/wiki/Live_Nation_Entertainment "Live Nation Entertainment") indicated that the response was the strongest he had experienced for a rugby match outside of internationals.[[112]](#cite_note-117)

In January 2023, construction began on [Te Kaha Stadium](/wiki/Te_Kaha_(stadium) "Te Kaha (stadium)") as a new facility for the city which can also be used as a home ground for the Crusaders. The stadium will seat 30,000 spectators, and will feature a fixed roof, becoming the second fully-covered stadium in New Zealand after [Forsyth Barr Stadium](/wiki/Forsyth_Barr_Stadium "Forsyth Barr Stadium") in [Dunedin](/wiki/Dunedin "Dunedin"). The current planned date for opening is April 2026.[[113]](#cite_note-118)

## Development programmes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Crusaders_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Development programmes")]

The Crusaders currently use their Academy to develop their players for the future and have had many players who have graduated from their academy and who have come through their system go on to play in [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby") and for the [All Blacks](/wiki/All_Blacks "All Blacks").[[114]](#cite_note-119) They use the Development XV (Crusaders Knights), Junior Crusaders (U18) and the Crusaders (U20) as a pathway to provide future players the opportunity to be able to play professional rugby.[[115]](#cite_note-120)[[116]](#cite_note-121)[[117]](#cite_note-122) They also have an International Academy Players Programme that primarily focuses on selecting young, up and coming players from around the globe and provides them with the opportunity to travel to [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), to learn and gain more experience about rugby in the Crusaders environment.[[118]](#cite_note-123)

It was played as a round robin competition, with no finals. **[^4](#ref_Four)** *Super Rugby Aotearoa adopted the same format in 2021 as the inaugural tournament in 2020, with the addition of a final between the top two ranked teams at the conclusion of the round robin stage.*[[123]](#cite_note-128)  
**[^5](#ref_Five)** *Super Rugby Trans Tasman was a crossover competition between the teams involved in [Super Rugby Aotearoa](/wiki/2021_Super_Rugby_Aotearoa_season "2021 Super Rugby Aotearoa season") and [Super Rugby AU](/wiki/2021_Super_Rugby_AU_season "2021 Super Rugby AU season"). Each team from Super Rugby AU played each team from Super Rugby Aotearoa once, and vice versa. A final was played between the top two seeded teams at the conclusion of the round robin matches.*[[124]](#cite_note-129)[[125]](#cite_note-130)

In addition to winning more Super Rugby titles than any other team, the franchise also holds several [competition records](/wiki/List_of_Super_Rugby_records "List of Super Rugby records"), including most points in a game and most [tries](/wiki/Try_(rugby) "Try (rugby)") in a game, both achieved in their 96–19 victory over the Waratahs in 2002. The Crusaders also hold the record for the fewest points scored in a game when they were defeated by the [Highlanders](/wiki/Highlanders_(rugby) "Highlanders (rugby)") 6–0 in 2009. They have also scored the most points in a season (677) in 2018 and most tries in a season (94) in 2018.

Individual players also hold records: [Andrew Mehrtens](/wiki/Andrew_Mehrtens "Andrew Mehrtens") for most points in a Super 12 season (206 in 1998), and [Rico Gear](/wiki/Rico_Gear "Rico Gear") for most tries in a season (15 in 2005). Twenty Four players have played over 100 games for the franchise: [Wyatt Crockett](/wiki/Wyatt_Crockett "Wyatt Crockett"), [Samuel Whitelock](/wiki/Sam_Whitelock "Sam Whitelock"), [Kieran Read](/wiki/Kieran_Read "Kieran Read"), [Andy Ellis](/wiki/Andy_Ellis_(rugby_union) "Andy Ellis (rugby union)"), [Owen Franks](/wiki/Owen_Franks "Owen Franks"), [Ryan Crotty](/wiki/Ryan_Crotty "Ryan Crotty"), [Corey Flynn](/wiki/Corey_Flynn "Corey Flynn"), [Richie McCaw](/wiki/Richie_McCaw "Richie McCaw"), [Dan Carter](/wiki/Dan_Carter "Dan Carter"), [Matt Todd](/wiki/Matt_Todd "Matt Todd"), [Luke Romano](/wiki/Luke_Romano "Luke Romano"), [Mitchell Drummond](/wiki/Mitchell_Drummond "Mitchell Drummond"), [Codie Taylor](/wiki/Codie_Taylor "Codie Taylor"), [Rueben Thorne](/wiki/Reuben_Thorne "Reuben Thorne"), [Caleb Ralph](/wiki/Caleb_Ralph "Caleb Ralph"), [Leon MacDonald](/wiki/Leon_MacDonald "Leon MacDonald"), [David Havili](/wiki/David_Havili "David Havili"), [Joe Moody](/wiki/Joe_Moody_(rugby_union) "Joe Moody (rugby union)"), [Greg Somerville](/wiki/Greg_Somerville "Greg Somerville"), [Chris Jack](/wiki/Chris_Jack "Chris Jack"), [Scott Barrett](/wiki/Scott_Barrett_(rugby_union) "Scott Barrett (rugby union)"), [Richie Mo'unga](/wiki/Richie_Mo%27unga "Richie Mo'unga"), [Justin Marshall](/wiki/Justin_Marshall "Justin Marshall"), [Jordan Taufua](/wiki/Jordan_Taufua "Jordan Taufua"). The Crusaders have also had three [International Rugby Board](/wiki/International_Rugby_Board "International Rugby Board") Players of the Year: [Dan Carter](/wiki/Dan_Carter "Dan Carter") (2005), (2012), (2015), [Richie McCaw](/wiki/Richie_McCaw "Richie McCaw") (2006), (2009), (2010) and [Kieran Read](/wiki/Kieran_Read "Kieran Read") (2013).

Half-back Justin Marshall was the first player to achieve the feat, playing for the Crusaders between 1996 and 2005.[[130]](#cite_note-MarshallAB-138) Both Thorne and Ralph achieved the 100-game mark at the same time. However, Ralph had played nine of his matches for other franchises: three for the Chiefs and six for the Blues.[[131]](#cite_note-Thorne100-139)[[132]](#cite_note-Ralph100-140) Ralph achieved the "100 Crusaders games" feat later that season; remarkably, they were played consecutively.[[133]](#cite_note-100Consecutive-141) Greg Somerville became the next player to contribute 100 games for the franchise, achieving this in the 2006 semi-final against the Bulls.[[134]](#cite_note-CrusadersFull-142) The first match of the 2008 season was Leon MacDonald's 100th game,[[135]](#cite_note-MacDonald100-143) and on 11 March 2011 Chris Jack started against the Brumbies to play his 100th match for the Crusaders.[[136]](#cite_note-144) The Crusaders also boast three winners of the [IRB International Player of the Year](/wiki/IRB_International_Player_of_the_Year "IRB International Player of the Year") Award: [Dan Carter](/wiki/Dan_Carter "Dan Carter") (2005, 2012 and 2015), [Kieran Read](/wiki/Kieran_Read "Kieran Read") (2013) and Richie McCaw (2006, 2009 and 2010).[[137]](#cite_note-2006IRBaward-145)

### 1996–2005 Team of the decade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Crusaders_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: 1996–2005 Team of the decade")]

On the eve of the last Super 12 final in 2005, a panel of experts picked the Crusaders team of the decade, which was published by the Christchurch Press. The experts chosen to select the team were: [Tane Norton](/wiki/Tane_Norton "Tane Norton") (former All Blacks captain), Vance Stewart (first Crusaders coach), Dick Tayler (president of supporters club), Bob Schumacher (former Christchurch Press rugby writer) and Tony Smith (Christchurch Press rugby writer).[[138]](#cite_note-TeamofDecade-146)

The most notable omissions from the team were [Dan Carter](/wiki/Dan_Carter "Dan Carter"), Richard Loe, and Reuben Thorne.[[138]](#cite_note-TeamofDecade-146) Daniel Carter was omitted due to the strength of Andrew Mehrtens and Aaron Mauger who occupied the [first](/wiki/Fly-half_(rugby_union) "Fly-half (rugby union)") and [second five-eighth](/wiki/Centre_(rugby_union) "Centre (rugby union)") positions respectively, and because he was regarded as "the star of the next decade". Despite being considered one of Canterbury's best ever props, Richard Loe missed out as he was only in the team for the unsuccessful 1996 season.[[138]](#cite_note-TeamofDecade-146) The former All Black captain Reuben Thorne was omitted due to the quality of players in the [lock](/wiki/Lock_(rugby_union) "Lock (rugby union)") and [blindside flanker](/wiki/Flanker_(rugby_union) "Flanker (rugby union)") positions. The most unlikely inclusion was for [Norm Berryman](/wiki/Norm_Berryman "Norm Berryman") at [right wing](/wiki/Wing_(rugby_union) "Wing (rugby union)").[[138]](#cite_note-TeamofDecade-146) Berryman was picked over Marika Vunibaka and Afato So'oalo due to his crucial tries late in the 1998 and 1999 seasons that helped the Crusaders qualify for the semi-finals.[[138]](#cite_note-TeamofDecade-146) The team:

## Coaches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Crusaders_(rugby_union)&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Coaches")]

The Crusaders were coached in their first season by [Vance Stewart](/wiki/Vance_Stewart "Vance Stewart"), who was assisted by [Aussie Mclean](/wiki/Brian_McLean_(rugby_union) "Brian McLean (rugby union)").[[9]](#cite_note-McIlraith321-10) Stewart was replaced by [Wayne Smith](/wiki/Wayne_Smith_(rugby_union) "Wayne Smith (rugby union)") in 1997; McLean too was replaced, by Peter Sloane. Smith continued as coach until he was appointed All Blacks coach after the 1999 season. [Robbie Deans](/wiki/Robbie_Deans "Robbie Deans") took over the reins for the 2000 season. Deans has had several assistants: in 2000 and 2001 his assistant was [Steve Hansen](/wiki/Steve_Hansen "Steve Hansen"); in 2002, [Colin Cooper](/wiki/Colin_Cooper_(rugby_union) "Colin Cooper (rugby union)"); in 2003 and 2004, Don Hayes; and in 2005 and 2006, [Vern Cotter](/wiki/Vern_Cotter "Vern Cotter").[[9]](#cite_note-McIlraith321-10) Deans' assistant for the 2007 season was former Crusader [Mark Hammett](/wiki/Mark_Hammett "Mark Hammett").[[143]](#cite_note-HammettAssist-151) Deans was appointed coach of [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_union_team "Australia national rugby union team") in December 2007, but was allowed to continue coaching the Crusaders in the 2008 season. Former captain, [Todd Blackadder](/wiki/Todd_Blackadder "Todd Blackadder") was appointed his successor in July 2008, with Hammett continuing as assistant coach.[[144]](#cite_note-BlackadderCoach-152) Hammett was appointed Hurricanes coach from the beginning of the 2011 season,[[145]](#cite_note-153) and consequently former prop [Dave Hewett](/wiki/Dave_Hewett "Dave Hewett") was appointed assistant forwards coach.[[146]](#cite_note-154) In June 2016, [Scott Robertson](/wiki/Scott_Robertson_(rugby_union) "Scott Robertson (rugby union)"), former Crusaders player and former head coach of the [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury_Rugby_Football_Union "Canterbury Rugby Football Union") National Provincial team and the [New Zealand Under 20](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_under-20_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national under-20 rugby union team") rugby team, was announced as the Crusaders head coach from 2017, and finished his tenure with the team in 2023.[[147]](#cite_note-155)[[148]](#cite_note-156) On June 6, 2023, [Rob Penney](/wiki/Rob_Penney "Rob Penney") was announced as the replacement for Scott Robertson as head coach of the Crusaders. He is assisted by former Crusaders Flanker [Matt Todd](/wiki/Matt_Todd "Matt Todd") from 2024 onwards.[[88]](#cite_note-coaching-92)[[149]](#cite_note-157)
